text: In this video, we demonstrate single incision laparoscopic clockwise continuous total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy for ulcerative colitis. The patient is a sixty-eight year old man with medically refractory ulcerative colitis for several years. Colonoscopy revealed inflammation from the anus to the splenic flexure, secondary to left-sided colitis and dysplasia in the distal sigmoid. This surgery is the first of three stage pouch surgeries.

A three-centimeter incision is made in the right lower quadrant, excising a disc-shaped section of skin. The surgeon enters the abdomen through a muscle-splitting incision and establishes pneumoperitoneum using a single port gel point system. The small bowel is inspected, and the right colon is identified from the ileocolic pedicle, which is then divided.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral separation of the right colon in a cephalad direction, detaching lateral attachments. The hepatic flexure and terminal ileum are mobilized, and the omentum is dissected off the transverse colon. The middle colic vessels are divided, preserving the duodenum. The splenic flexure is taken down, extending dissection to the left colon.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectosigmoid colon and staples across the rectosigmoid junction in the distal sigmoid area, ensuring the region is well vascularized and positioned in the pelvis. The stapled end is grasped, and the entire specimen is exteriorized through the right lower quadrant stoma site. The area is narrowed using zero polysorb sutures, and the stoma is matured in place.

The resected specimen includes the terminal ileum and colon, visibly inflamed from the left side extending to the hepatic flexure. The surgical procedure involved a single incision laparoscopic total abdominal colectomy with ileostomy for ulcerative colitis.
